problem.statement: There are `n` courses labeled `0..n-1`. You are given `m` prerequisite pairs `(a, b)` meaning course `b` must be completed before course `a`. Print `true` if it is possible to finish **all** courses, or `false` if the prerequisites form a cycle that makes this impossible.

Given an array of integers, sort it in **ascending order** using **Heap Sort** and print the sorted array.

Print space-separated integers on one line.

Given an undirected graph with `n` nodes (0-indexed) and `m` edges (possibly disconnected), print `true` if the graph is **bipartite** (2-colorable so no edge connects two same-colored nodes), else `false`.
Given a binary tree, print its **level-order traversal**: one line per depth level, values space-separated top to bottom.

Given a `rows x cols` binary matrix (only `0`/`1` entries), print the **area of the largest rectangle** that contains only `1`s. Solve by treating each row as the base of a histogram (accumulating consecutive 1-heights column-by-column from the row above) and applying the largest-rectangle-in-histogram technique to each row's histogram.

Given an array of integers, sort it in **ascending order** using **Odd-Even Sort** and print the sorted array.

Print space-separated integers on one line.

Given an array of integers, sort it in **ascending order** using **Radix Sort (LSD)** and print the sorted array.

Print space-separated integers on one line.

A grid has cells: `0` empty, `1` fresh orange, `2` rotten orange. Every minute, a rotten orange rots each 4-directionally adjacent fresh orange. Print the **number of minutes** until no fresh orange remains, or **-1** if some fresh orange can never rot.

Given an array of integers, sort it in **ascending order** using **Shell Sort** and print the sorted array.

Print space-separated integers on one line.

Given an array of integers, sort it in **ascending order** using **Stooge Sort** and print the sorted array.

Print space-separated integers on one line.

Given an array of integers, sort it in **ascending order** using **Tim Sort** and print the sorted array.

Print space-separated integers on one line.
Given a string `s`, print its **Z-array**: `Z[i]` is the length of the longest substring starting at `i` that is also a prefix of `s` (by convention `Z[0] = 0`), space-separated.
